Source_Species.reason: The mutant was generated from SaDAH, where SaDAH is from Sinomenium acutum.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Sinomenium acutum is explicitly named as the source of SaDAH.

Applied_Species.reason: SaDAH was heterologously expressed in Escherichia coli for recombinant protein production.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Escherichia coli is explicitly stated as the heterologous expression host.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: The verified construct was transformed into E. coli BL21 (DE3).

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: DAH is described as a Fe(II)- and 2-oxoglutarate-dependent halogenase of the 2ODD/2ODH-related family; the G226D form is its mutant variant.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: In vitro assays for SaDAH-G226D included Fe(II), 2OG, and NaCl in Tris buffer.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: The paper explicitly describes site-directed mutagenesis, recombinant expression/purification, in vitro enzyme assays, and LC-HRAM-MS/LC-MS analysis.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The mutant assay concerns conversion of dechloroacutumine, yielding predominantly 11-hydroxy-dechloroacutumine; acutumine is discussed as the native terminal product and minor chlorination product in Fig. 2c text.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Acutumine and dechloroacutumine derivatives are alkaloids in the paper.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The Results state the G226D mutant is devoid of halogenase activity and instead exhibits exclusive/predominant hydroxylase activity toward dechloroacutumine, showing the role of Gly226.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper discusses DAH as a starting point for directed evolution and metabolic engineering, but does not explicitly assign this mechanistic switching application specifically to the G226D mutant.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: The mutant loses native halogenase activity, which is the stated tradeoff.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The statement that the mutant 'predominantly produced 11-hydroxy-dechloroacutumine' is supported by Fig. 2c/caption, but 'chlorination abolished/undetectable as functional output of DAH' overstates the text because Fig. 2 caption notes chlorination product 1 is also produced as a minor product, while the Results text says devoid of halogenase activity.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three points are consistent with the Results: G226D tests the unusual residue, shifts activity from halogenation to hydroxylation, and supports the role of the Asp-to-Gly substitution in enabling halogenation.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: GenBank accessions MT040708 and MT040709 are listed for the genes reported; McDAH is one of the two DAHs and corresponds to one accession, though the paper does not map accession-to-gene name inline. Given only two DAHs are reported, this is directly inferable but not explicitly assigned in text.

Source_Species.reason: McDAH is explicitly named as M. canadense dechloroacutumine halogenase.

Applied_Species.reason: McDAH was identified from M. canadense and shown in recombinant assays; recombinant McDAH activity is mentioned, but 'Menispermaceae plants' as applied species is broader than directly tested.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: McDAH itself is from M. canadense; Sinomenium acutum pertains to the ortholog SaDAH, not application of McDAH per se.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: McDAH is identified in the context of (−)-acutumine biosynthesis, which is part of benzylisoquinoline alkaloid specialized metabolism.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The enzyme is described as catalyzing the terminal chlorination step, i.e., the terminal halogenation branch of the acutumine pathway.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: The paper explicitly states chlorination is the terminal step and DAH catalyzes this terminal chlorination reaction.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: DAH directly converts dechloroacutumine to acutumine.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The gene is named dechloroacutumine halogenase.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: DAH is explicitly described as an Fe(II)- and 2-oxoglutarate-dependent halogenase related to the 2ODD family.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The chlorination reaction is directly shown; recombinant McDAH exhibits identical activity to SaDAH, and DAH generally is discussed as having trace hydroxylation and azidation capability.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: (−)-Acutumine is explicitly described as a tetracyclic chloroalkaloid.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The introduction states acutumine has selective cytotoxicity to cultured human T cells and memory-enhancing properties in Wistar rats.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: The paper states (−)-acutumine accumulation is relatively more enriched in roots than stems and leaves in M. canadense.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: The candidate gene has 459-fold higher transcript expression in roots than leaves in M. canadense.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Root enrichment is clearly stated for M. canadense metabolite accumulation, but not as a specific expression analysis across both applied species.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: DAH assays used Fe(II), 2OG, and NaCl; azidation was observed with NaN3.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: McDAH is presented as catalyzing the terminal chlorination step leading to the chloroalkaloid (−)-acutumine.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: Terminal chlorination is supported, but the paper does not explicitly call it rate-limiting or a committed control step.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The discussion explicitly mentions metabolic engineering, directed evolution, and production of new-to-nature plant natural products using DAH.

Omics_Data.reason: The listed SRA and TSA accessions appear in the Data availability section.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Comparative transcriptomics, tissue-specific expression, recombinant expression, in vitro assays with LC-MS, and phylogenetic analysis are all described; recombinant McDAH activity is also noted.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The Asp226Gly substitution, first plant-origin 2ODH claim, and absence of obvious DAH ortholog in S. japonica are all explicitly stated.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three summary statements are directly supported by the Results and figures.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: GenBank accessions MT040708 and MT040709 are listed for the reported genes; SaDAH is one of the two DAHs, though the text does not explicitly pair the accession with the gene name inline.

Source_Species.reason: SaDAH was identified from Sinomenium acutum transcriptome.

Applied_Species.reason: SaDAH is from S. acutum and was functionally tested after heterologous expression in E. coli.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Both Sinomenium acutum and Escherichia coli are explicitly mentioned in the study context.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: SaDAH is characterized in (−)-acutumine biosynthesis, a benzylisoquinoline alkaloid pathway context.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: SaDAH catalyzes the terminal chlorination branch/step.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: The paper explicitly says DAH catalyzes the terminal chlorination step.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: SaDAH directly converts dechloroacutumine to acutumine.

Enzyme_Name.reason: SaDAH is identified as dechloroacutumine halogenase.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: It is explicitly a Fe(II)- and 2-oxoglutarate-dependent halogenase related to 2ODDs.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The chlorination reaction, trace hydroxylation, and azidation in presence of azide are all described and supported by Fig. 2 and supplementary figures.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The product linked to the pathway is (−)-acutumine.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper attributes selective cytotoxicity and memory-enhancing properties to (−)-acutumine.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Root-enriched accumulation is shown for M. canadense, but the paper does not explicitly state tissue accumulation site for S. acutum.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: SaDAH was identified in S. acutum transcriptome and reported to have 99.1% identity to McDAH, making it a likely ortholog; this is explicitly stated.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: SaDAH chlorination required 2OG/Fe(II)/NaCl and azidation occurred with NaN3; lack of activity on non-native alkaloids is also reported.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: SaDAH directly converts dechloroacutumine to acutumine and also catalyzes azidation under azide supplementation.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: It is terminal and determines chlorinated product identity, but the paper does not explicitly call it rate-limiting or a control step.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The discussion explicitly proposes DAH for derivatization, directed evolution, and metabolic engineering applications.

Omics_Data.reason: The cited SRA and TSA accessions are listed in Data availability.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Recombinant purification from E. coli, in vitro assays, LC-HRAM-MS, kinetic assays, and substrate/anion testing are all explicitly described.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The kinetic constants, substrate specificity, and claim as first biochemically characterized plant 2ODH are all supported by the Results and supplementary references cited in text.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each of the three summary points is directly supported by the Results section.
